description:
Goal: execute compiler2 macro functions as IR-lowered compile-time Fz code over the same Fz-shaped quoted AST values produced by the parser and consumed by source production, and explicitly wire compile-time compiler-host callbacks (`Fz.Compiler`) through that runtime so running macro code can call back into the publication boundary from `.11.7.4`.

Signal:
- a local `defmacro inc(x) do quote do: unquote(x) + 1 end end` shares the ordinary function lowering/IR substrate, runs during source production, and returns a new AST root in the same source heap
- macro functions receive `__CALLER__` plus quoted argument roots as ordinary Fz values (`AnyValueRef` roots to tuples/lists/atoms/maps/etc.); args are not evaluated and are not decoded to Rust AST first
- macro return values are `AnyValueRef` roots in the active compile-time process heap; recursive expansion consumes the returned root directly and derives source identity before publishing facts
- the active compile-time interpreter run carries compiler-host context plus the current scope/publication session, so running macro code can call back into compiler2 without a second authority path
- `Fz.Compiler` exists as a compile-time runtime-library/extern surface that accepts AST roots as ordinary Fz `AnyValueRef` values and routes them into the same publication boundary from `.11.7.4`
- quote/unquote lower as compile-time source-value construction/splice operations over the compiler2 source schema; they do not call the old AST codec and do not allocate into a second AST representation
- macro execution errors surface through compiler2 diagnostics/telemetry with call-site and definition context
- no fz2 path calls `frontend::macros::expand_program_with_types`, `exec::ast_value`, or the scratch `CompileTimeEvaluator`
- there is no separate macro-only lowering implementation; the work graph exposes macro-executable readiness from the shared lowering/IR path
- ordinary runtime/native execution paths do not gain compiler authority or `Fz.Compiler` behavior

Change:
- reuse compiler2's function lowering / lowered-body / IR substrate for macros, adding only the compile-time ABI, quote/unquote lowering, and compile-time-only host surface required for macro execution
- add a compile-time macro-executable readiness fact over that shared substrate so source production can wait for specific macro functions after `ModuleDefined`
- make runtime-only consumers (root seeding, dispatch/semantic closure, artifact/native lowering) ignore macro defs instead of forcing the lowerer itself to reject them
- run macro calls through `ir_interp` on the active compile-time `Process`; the process supplies the heap/world, so the Fz-visible ABI passes roots, not heap handles
- define the macro ABI as `(__CALLER__root, quoted_arg_root...) -> returned_root`; every root is an `AnyValueRef` valid in the current process heap
- add compile-time host context to the interpreter/runtime so a running macro has access to the live compiler2 world/session it is expanding within
- add the compile-time `Fz.Compiler` runtime-library surface and interpreter extern dispatch here, wired to the source-publication boundary from `.11.7.4`; normal runtime/native code must not gain compiler authority
- implement quote/unquote over the compiler2 Fz-shaped quoted source value model in-place on the active source heap
- validate and root returned/source-submitted AST values before recursive expansion continues, without decoding them through a Rust AST representation or treating raw heap address as source identity
- reject or diagnose any attempt to use a root that is not valid for the active process heap/world
- define deterministic macro execution limits/signals for recursion/runaway expansion
- add telemetry for macro lowering/execution/host-service calls carrying raw ids/source-root identities/source facts, not formatted strings

Why:
- Elixir's model is that macros are compiled functions with a caller/env argument and quoted args; the difference is call context and compiler authority, not a second body shape
- the old evaluator proves the semantics, but its value model and whole-program preload are the wrong substrate
- this is the correct seam for `Fz.Compiler`: `.4` owns compiler publication, `.5` owns the runtime/interpreter path that lets executing macro code call back into that publication boundary
- if we fork macro lowering from ordinary lowering, or smear compiler-host wiring back into `.4`, we duplicate one of the core compiler seams right where we are trying to simplify it
title: Compiler2 macro runtime: shared lowering/IR plus Fz.Compiler host callbacks
